question: You respond to a 67-year-old male at a grocery store complaining of chest pain. He is diaphoretic and pale with his hand clutched to his chest. He states the pain started 20 minutes ago while he was shopping and describes it as a heavy pressure radiating to his left arm. His wife states he has a history of hypertension and takes lisinopril and amlodipine daily. Vital signs are BP 158/94 HR 88 RR 20 SpO2 96% on room air. 12-lead ECG shows ST elevation in leads V1-V4. After applying oxygen and establishing IV access you administer 324 mg of aspirin. The patient asks why he needs to chew the aspirin instead of swallowing it whole. What is the correct explanation?
option_a: Chewing releases a mint flavor that reduces nausea
option_b: Chewing allows faster absorption through buccal mucosa and faster onset of antiplatelet effects
option_c: Chewing prevents the aspirin from causing gastric ulcers
option_d: Chewing is required because the patient may lose consciousness and aspirate a whole tablet
rationale: Chewing aspirin allows faster absorption and more rapid inhibition of platelet aggregation compared to swallowing whole tablets. In acute coronary syndrome time to antiplatelet effect is critical. Chewed aspirin reaches peak plasma levels in about 15 minutes versus 60 minutes for swallowed tablets.

question: You are treating a 54-year-old female who called 911 for chest pain that woke her from sleep at 3 AM. She describes substernal pressure rated 8 out of 10 with associated shortness of breath and nausea. She has a history of type 2 diabetes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Her medications include metformin glipizide lisinopril and atorvastatin. Vital signs are BP 168/96 HR 102 RR 22 SpO2 94% on room air. 12-lead shows ST elevation in II III and aVF with ST depression in aVL. You have given aspirin and established IV access. You prepare to administer nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual. Before administration what critical question must you ask this patient?
option_a: Have you taken any erectile dysfunction medications in the past 48 hours
option_b: Have you eaten anything in the past 6 hours
option_c: Are you allergic to nitrates
option_d: Have you ever had a heart attack before
rationale: Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors such as sildenafil tadalafil and vardenafil potentiate the hypotensive effects of nitrates and can cause profound life-threatening hypotension. Sildenafil and vardenafil contraindicate nitrates for 24 hours and tadalafil for 48 hours due to its longer half-life. This is true for both male and female patients as these medications are also used for pulmonary hypertension.

question: A 71-year-old male with a history of inferior wall MI three years ago presents with chest pain and dyspnea. He states he took three of his own nitroglycerin tablets at home without relief. His wife says he has been increasingly short of breath over the past week and has gained 8 pounds. On exam you note jugular venous distension bilateral lower extremity edema and crackles in both lung bases. Vital signs are BP 88/62 HR 110 RR 28 SpO2 88% on room air. 12-lead shows ST elevation in II III and aVF with right-sided leads showing ST elevation in V4R. Given this complete clinical picture why is nitroglycerin relatively contraindicated in this patient?
option_a: Nitroglycerin will worsen his crackles
option_b: Nitroglycerin will cause reflex tachycardia and increase oxygen demand
option_c: Right ventricular infarction is preload-dependent and nitroglycerin reduces preload causing profound hypotension
option_d: Nitroglycerin is ineffective after home nitroglycerin has been taken
rationale: Right ventricular infarction makes the heart dependent on adequate preload to maintain cardiac output. The infarcted RV cannot increase contractility to compensate for reduced filling. Nitroglycerin causes venodilation reducing preload which can cause severe hypotension in RV infarct. Treatment includes IV fluids to maintain preload and avoiding nitrates and diuretics.

question: You respond to a 45-year-old male who collapsed at a construction site. Coworkers state he complained of palpitations and dizziness before losing consciousness. He is now awake but confused. The cardiac monitor shows a wide complex tachycardia at a rate of 210 with a regular rhythm. Vital signs are BP 78/50 HR 210 RR 24 SpO2 94%. The patient is diaphoretic with cool clammy skin. You recognize this as hemodynamically unstable ventricular tachycardia. While preparing for synchronized cardioversion you establish IV access. What medication should you administer to provide sedation before cardioversion?
option_a: Adenosine 6 mg IV push
option_b: Midazolam 2-5 mg IV or Etomidate 0.1-0.2 mg/kg IV
option_c: Amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 minutes
option_d: Atropine 0.5 mg IV
rationale: Synchronized cardioversion is painful and patients who are conscious should receive procedural sedation if time and hemodynamic status permit. Midazolam or etomidate provide rapid onset sedation. Adenosine is not used for VT. Amiodarone is for stable VT or refractory VF. Atropine is for bradycardia. However in severely unstable patients cardioversion should not be delayed for sedation.

question: A 62-year-old female presents with sudden onset of palpitations and lightheadedness while at dinner. She has a history of mitral valve prolapse but takes no daily medications. She is alert and oriented but anxious. The cardiac monitor shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 188 beats per minute with no visible P waves. Vital signs are BP 102/68 HR 188 RR 18 SpO2 98% on room air. Vagal maneuvers have failed to convert the rhythm. You prepare to administer adenosine. What is the correct dose route and administration technique for adenosine in this patient?
option_a: Adenosine 6 mg slow IV push over 1-2 minutes through antecubital IV followed by saline flush
option_b: Adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push through proximal IV site immediately followed by 20 mL rapid saline flush and arm elevation
option_c: Adenosine 12 mg IV push followed by 10 mL saline flush
option_d: Adenosine 6 mg IV push repeated every 5 minutes until conversion
rationale: Adenosine has an extremely short half-life of less than 10 seconds and must be administered as a rapid IV push followed immediately by a 20 mL saline flush using a stopcock or push-pull technique. The IV should be as proximal as possible to ensure the drug reaches the heart before degradation. Arm elevation helps with rapid delivery. The initial dose is 6 mg with 12 mg for subsequent doses if needed.

question: You are called to a 78-year-old female at a nursing home who staff reports has been increasingly confused over the past two days. She has a history of atrial fibrillation and heart failure. Her medications include digoxin furosemide potassium chloride and metoprolol. On exam she is oriented only to person with visual hallucinations. She complains of nausea and seeing yellow halos around lights. Vital signs are BP 104/62 HR 52 irregular RR 18 SpO2 95% on 2L NC. The cardiac monitor shows atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response and frequent PVCs including some in a bigeminal pattern. What is the most likely cause of this presentation?
option_a: Worsening heart failure
option_b: Stroke
option_c: Digoxin toxicity
option_d: Urinary tract infection with sepsis
rationale: This presentation is classic digoxin toxicity featuring GI symptoms such as nausea and vomiting visual disturbances particularly yellow-green halos called xanthopsia and cardiac arrhythmias. The combination of atrial fibrillation with slow response and increased ectopy particularly bigeminy is characteristic. Elderly patients and those with renal impairment or electrolyte imbalances are at increased risk. Treatment includes withholding digoxin and digoxin-specific antibody fragments for severe toxicity.

question: Continuing with the previous patient who you suspect has digoxin toxicity her rhythm suddenly changes to a wide complex tachycardia and she becomes unresponsive. Her BP is now 60/40 and she has weak pulses. The monitor shows what appears to be bidirectional ventricular tachycardia at 160 bpm. What is the treatment of choice for this life-threatening digoxin-induced arrhythmia?
option_a: Synchronized cardioversion at 100 joules
option_b: Amiodarone 300 mg IV push
option_c: Digoxin immune Fab fragments
option_d: Calcium chloride 1 gram IV
rationale: Digoxin immune Fab also known as Digibind or DigiFab is the specific antidote for severe digoxin toxicity with life-threatening arrhythmias. It binds free digoxin and is excreted renally. Cardioversion may be ineffective and can precipitate refractory arrhythmias in digoxin toxicity. Calcium is contraindicated as it can worsen digoxin-induced arrhythmias. The dose is based on estimated digoxin body load or empirically 10-20 vials for acute severe toxicity.

question: A 58-year-old male presents with crushing chest pain of 45 minutes duration. He is diaphoretic pale and clutching his chest. History includes hypertension diabetes and he underwent coronary stent placement 6 months ago. His medications include aspirin clopidogrel metoprolol lisinopril metformin and atorvastatin. Vital signs are BP 146/92 HR 96 RR 22 SpO2 95% on room air. 12-lead ECG shows 4 mm ST elevation in V2-V5 with reciprocal depression inferiorly. The cath lab is 45 minutes away. Medical control asks if the patient is a thrombolytic candidate. What is the PRIMARY contraindication to thrombolytic therapy in this patient?
option_a: His history of diabetes mellitus
option_b: His current use of clopidogrel
option_c: His coronary stent was placed only 6 months ago
option_d: Recent PCI with stent placement is not a contraindication and he may be a thrombolytic candidate if no other contraindications exist
rationale: Prior PCI with stent placement is not a contraindication to thrombolytic therapy. Absolute contraindications include prior intracranial hemorrhage known cerebrovascular malformation active internal bleeding suspected aortic dissection significant head trauma within 3 months and ischemic stroke within 3 months. Being on antiplatelet therapy like clopidogrel increases bleeding risk but is not an absolute contraindication. Primary PCI is preferred over thrombolytics when available within 120 minutes.

question: A 24-year-old female college student presents with acute shortness of breath and wheezing that began 30 minutes ago after she was exposed to a cat at her friend's apartment. She has a history of asthma and uses an albuterol inhaler as needed. She states she used her inhaler twice without relief before calling 911. On exam she is sitting upright using accessory muscles to breathe. You hear diffuse expiratory wheezing bilaterally. She is able to speak in short sentences only. Vital signs are BP 128/78 HR 118 RR 28 SpO2 91% on room air. After applying oxygen you prepare to administer a nebulized bronchodilator. What is the appropriate dose of albuterol for nebulization in this patient?
option_a: Albuterol 0.5 mg in 3 mL normal saline over 10-15 minutes
option_b: Albuterol 2.5 mg in 3 mL normal saline over 5-15 minutes and may repeat every 20 minutes or give continuously
option_c: Albuterol 5 mg in 10 mL normal saline over 30 minutes
option_d: Albuterol 10 mg IV push over 2 minutes
rationale: The standard nebulized albuterol dose for acute bronchospasm is 2.5 mg diluted in 3 mL normal saline delivered over 5-15 minutes. This can be repeated every 20 minutes for up to 3 doses or given as continuous nebulization in severe cases. Albuterol is a beta-2 agonist that causes bronchial smooth muscle relaxation. Side effects include tachycardia tremor and hypokalemia.

question: You continue treating the 24-year-old asthmatic patient. After two albuterol treatments her wheezing has improved slightly but she remains in moderate respiratory distress with SpO2 of 92% on 15L non-rebreather. Her vital signs are now BP 132/80 HR 132 RR 26. Your medical protocols allow for administration of ipratropium bromide in combination with albuterol for refractory bronchospasm. What is the mechanism of action and appropriate dose of ipratropium?
option_a: Ipratropium is a beta-2 agonist given as 0.5 mg nebulized to enhance albuterol effects
option_b: Ipratropium is an anticholinergic that blocks muscarinic receptors causing bronchodilation given as 0.5 mg nebulized with albuterol
option_c: Ipratropium is a corticosteroid that reduces inflammation given as 0.25 mg nebulized
option_d: Ipratropium is an antihistamine that blocks bronchospasm given as 1 mg nebulized
rationale: Ipratropium bromide is an anticholinergic agent that blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors in bronchial smooth muscle causing bronchodilation. Unlike beta-agonists it works through a different mechanism providing additive bronchodilation when combined with albuterol. The dose is 0.5 mg or 500 mcg nebulized typically combined with albuterol in the same nebulizer. It has a slower onset than albuterol but provides additional benefit in moderate to severe asthma exacerbations.

question: A 68-year-old male with COPD presents with severe dyspnea and increased sputum production over the past 3 days. He ran out of his home oxygen two days ago. On exam he has barrel chest diminished breath sounds diffusely with scattered rhonchi and is speaking in one to two word sentences. He is using pursed-lip breathing and tripod positioning. Vital signs are BP 152/88 HR 108 RR 32 SpO2 78% on room air. After placing him on a non-rebreather at 15 LPM his SpO2 rises to 94% but he becomes increasingly lethargic. What is the most likely explanation for his deteriorating mental status despite improved oxygenation?
option_a: He is having a stroke
option_b: The non-rebreather is defective
option_c: High-flow oxygen has eliminated his hypoxic drive and he is now retaining CO2 causing CO2 narcosis
option_d: He is developing pulmonary edema from the oxygen
rationale: Some patients with chronic COPD develop chronic CO2 retention and their respiratory drive becomes dependent on hypoxia rather than hypercapnia. Administering high-flow oxygen eliminates this hypoxic drive and can cause hypoventilation with worsening hypercapnia leading to CO2 narcosis manifesting as somnolence and altered mental status. Treatment includes reducing oxygen to target SpO2 88-92% and may require ventilatory support with BiPAP. However never withhold oxygen from a hypoxic patient.

question: A 34-year-old female presents with sudden onset severe difficulty breathing while eating dinner at a restaurant. Her husband states she was eating shrimp when she suddenly grabbed her throat developed hives on her face and chest and began wheezing. On exam she has diffuse urticaria angioedema of the lips and tongue audible stridor and diffuse wheezing. She is extremely anxious and can only speak one word at a time between gasps. Vital signs are BP 82/50 HR 138 RR 36 SpO2 84% on room air. You recognize anaphylactic shock. What is the first medication dose and route you should administer?
option_a: Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV push
option_b: Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV
option_c: Epinephrine 1 mg of 1:10000 concentration IV push
option_d: Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1000 concentration IM into the lateral thigh
rationale: Epinephrine is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis and should be administered immediately. The correct dose for anaphylaxis is 0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1000 concentration given intramuscularly in the lateral thigh. The 1:10000 concentration IV is reserved for cardiac arrest not anaphylaxis. IM injection in the thigh provides rapid absorption. Antihistamines and steroids are adjunctive treatments but should never delay epinephrine. The dose can be repeated every 5-15 minutes if needed.

question: You continue treating the 34-year-old in anaphylaxis. You have administered IM epinephrine 0.5 mg and her wheezing has improved slightly but she remains hypotensive with BP 76/48. You have established two large-bore IVs and are giving a fluid bolus. Her husband mentions she takes propranolol for anxiety. After a second dose of IM epinephrine she remains hypotensive and bradycardic at 58 bpm. What medication should you consider for this patient with anaphylaxis refractory to epinephrine who is on a beta-blocker?
option_a: Higher doses of IM epinephrine up to 2 mg
option_b: Glucagon 1-5 mg IV because it bypasses blocked beta receptors
option_c: Atropine 1 mg IV for the bradycardia
option_d: Norepinephrine drip at 2-20 mcg per minute
rationale: Patients on beta-blockers may have anaphylaxis that is refractory to epinephrine because epinephrine's beta-adrenergic effects are blocked. Glucagon activates adenylyl cyclase through a non-beta-receptor mechanism increasing cAMP and providing inotropic and chronotropic effects that bypass the beta-blockade. The dose is 1-5 mg IV followed by infusion of 5-15 mcg per minute if effective. Vasopressin is another alternative in beta-blocker resistant anaphylaxis.

question: A 42-year-old male construction worker fell 15 feet from scaffolding landing on his right side. He is alert and oriented complaining of severe right-sided chest pain worse with breathing and right hip pain. On exam he has tenderness over the right lateral ribs with crepitus and deformity of the right hip with the leg shortened and externally rotated. He rates his pain as 10 out of 10. Vital signs are BP 138/86 HR 112 RR 24 shallow SpO2 96% on room air. You establish IV access and prepare to administer pain medication. What is an appropriate initial dose of fentanyl for this patient?
option_a: Fentanyl 250 mcg IV push
option_b: Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg IV or 25-100 mcg IV titrated to pain relief
option_c: Fentanyl 500 mcg IM
option_d: Fentanyl 50 mcg/kg IV push
rationale: Fentanyl is an excellent choice for trauma pain because it provides rapid analgesia with minimal hemodynamic effects compared to morphine. The appropriate dose is 1-2 mcg/kg IV which for an average adult is approximately 50-100 mcg. It should be titrated in increments every 3-5 minutes until adequate pain relief is achieved. Fentanyl has a rapid onset of 1-2 minutes IV and duration of 30-60 minutes. Respiratory depression and hypotension are potential side effects that require monitoring.

question: A 67-year-old female presents with severe abdominal pain that began 6 hours ago. She describes the pain as constant and diffuse rated 9 out of 10 with nausea and one episode of vomiting. She has a history of atrial fibrillation and takes warfarin and diltiazem. On exam her abdomen is diffusely tender with guarding but is soft. Bowel sounds are hypoactive. Vital signs are BP 108/62 HR 102 irregular RR 22 SpO2 97% on room air. You are concerned for mesenteric ischemia given her history of atrial fibrillation. While transporting you wish to provide pain relief. Why might morphine be a less optimal choice than fentanyl in this patient?
option_a: Morphine is contraindicated in patients on warfarin
option_b: Morphine causes histamine release leading to vasodilation and potential hypotension which could worsen mesenteric perfusion
option_c: Morphine is ineffective for abdominal pain
option_d: Morphine causes increased bowel motility which could worsen her condition
rationale: Morphine causes histamine release which leads to vasodilation and potential hypotension. In a patient with suspected mesenteric ischemia who is already borderline hypotensive with signs of potential shock this could worsen gut perfusion. Fentanyl does not cause significant histamine release and is more hemodynamically stable. Both are effective for visceral pain. Additionally morphine can cause sphincter of Oddi spasm though this is more relevant in biliary disease.

question: A 28-year-old female presents with a severe migraine headache that began 8 hours ago. She describes throbbing unilateral pain rated 8 out of 10 with photophobia phonophobia and nausea. She has vomited twice. She has a history of migraines and states this feels like her typical migraine but over-the-counter medications have not helped. She is lying in a dark room with a cold cloth over her eyes. Vital signs are BP 128/78 HR 88 RR 16 SpO2 99% on room air. Your protocols allow for ondansetron and ketorolac. What is the mechanism of action and dose of ketorolac for acute migraine?
option_a: Ketorolac is an opioid analgesic that binds mu receptors given as 10 mg IV
option_b: Ketorolac is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug that inhibits cyclooxygenase reducing prostaglandin synthesis given as 15-30 mg IV or IM
option_c: Ketorolac is a serotonin receptor agonist given as 60 mg IM
option_d: Ketorolac is a dopamine antagonist given as 30 mg IV
rationale: Ketorolac is an NSAID that provides analgesia and anti-inflammatory effects by inhibiting cyclooxygenase and reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is effective for migraine particularly when combined with antiemetics. The dose is 15-30 mg IV or 30-60 mg IM. Contraindications include renal impairment GI bleeding history active peptic ulcer disease coagulopathy and third trimester pregnancy. Ketorolac provides opioid-level analgesia without respiratory depression or sedation.

question: An 8-year-old male fell off his bicycle and has an obvious deformity of his left forearm. He is crying and holding his arm. His mother is present and very anxious. The arm is angulated with swelling but skin is intact and distal pulses are present. He rates his pain as very bad pointing to a pain scale showing 8 out of 10. He weighs approximately 25 kg. Vital signs are BP 104/68 HR 110 RR 22 SpO2 99% on room air. Your protocols allow intranasal fentanyl for pediatric pain management. What is the appropriate dose and administration technique?
option_a: Fentanyl 25 mcg IV push
option_b: Fentanyl 1.5-2 mcg/kg intranasally using atomizer device divided between both nares so approximately 37.5-50 mcg total
option_c: Fentanyl 100 mcg intranasally in one nare
option_d: Fentanyl 0.1 mcg/kg intranasally
rationale: Intranasal fentanyl provides rapid analgesia without the need for IV access making it ideal for pediatric pain management. The dose is 1.5-2 mcg/kg with a maximum of 100 mcg. Using the mucosal atomizer device the dose should be divided between both nares with no more than 0.5 mL per nare to ensure mucosal absorption. For a 25 kg child this would be approximately 37.5-50 mcg total. Onset is 5-10 minutes with peak effect at 15-20 minutes.

question: A 35-year-old male is found unresponsive in an alley by police. There is drug paraphernalia nearby including a syringe and burnt spoon. On exam he is unresponsive to painful stimuli with pinpoint pupils. His respiratory rate is 4 breaths per minute with shallow respirations and cyanotic lips. Vital signs are BP 90/60 HR 58 RR 4 SpO2 74% on room air. You immediately begin bag-valve-mask ventilation with supplemental oxygen. What is the appropriate initial dose of naloxone for this patient and what is the treatment goal?
option_a: Naloxone 2 mg IV push with the goal of complete arousal and normal mental status
option_b: Naloxone 0.4-2 mg IV/IM/IN titrated to adequate respiratory effort not necessarily full arousal
option_c: Naloxone 10 mg IV push to ensure complete reversal
option_d: Naloxone 0.04 mg IV to avoid precipitating withdrawal
rationale: The goal of naloxone administration in opioid overdose is to restore adequate spontaneous ventilation not necessarily full alertness. Starting with 0.4 mg and titrating up allows you to restore breathing while minimizing the risk of precipitating acute withdrawal which can cause vomiting aspiration agitation and seizures. The dose can be given IV IM IN or subcutaneously. Additional doses may be needed as naloxone has a shorter half-life than many opioids.

question: You successfully reversed the respiratory depression in the previous patient with naloxone 0.8 mg IV. He is now awake and breathing at 14 breaths per minute with SpO2 of 96%. He becomes agitated demanding to leave and stating he feels terrible. He is sweating and complaining of abdominal cramps. What is happening and how should you counsel this patient?
option_a: He is having an allergic reaction to naloxone and needs diphenhydramine
option_b: He is experiencing acute opioid withdrawal precipitated by naloxone and should be counseled that the naloxone will wear off in 30-90 minutes and he may become sedated again
option_c: The naloxone is not working and he needs more
option_d: He is having a heart attack from the naloxone
rationale: Naloxone competitively binds to opioid receptors displacing the opioid and reversing its effects. This can precipitate acute withdrawal in opioid-dependent individuals causing agitation diaphoresis nausea vomiting abdominal cramps piloerection and potentially aggression. More importantly naloxone has a shorter duration of action 30-90 minutes than most opioids so re-sedation is a significant risk. Patients need close monitoring and transport for observation. Many will want to refuse care but should be counseled about the risk of re-sedation and death.

question: A 32-year-old male is in the psychiatric unit of a hospital being transported to another facility. During transport he becomes extremely agitated believing the ambulance is a spaceship and you are aliens trying to abduct him. He is attempting to remove his seatbelts and has become combative striking your partner. Physical restraints are being applied. He has a history of schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. Vital signs before he became combative were BP 142/88 HR 118 RR 20. Your protocols allow for chemical sedation. What is an appropriate medication choice and dose for acute agitation in this patient?
option_a: Midazolam 5-10 mg IM alone
option_b: Ketamine 4-5 mg/kg IM for rapid sedation of severe agitation
option_c: Haloperidol 5 mg IM with diphenhydramine 50 mg IM to prevent dystonic reaction
option_d: Either B or C would be appropriate depending on severity with ketamine for severe agitation and haloperidol plus diphenhydramine for moderate agitation
rationale: Both options are appropriate depending on severity. Ketamine 4-5 mg/kg IM provides rapid dissociative sedation within 3-5 minutes and is excellent for severe agitation but requires airway monitoring. Haloperidol 5-10 mg IM with diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IM to prevent dystonic reactions is a classic combination for moderate psychotic agitation. Droperidol and newer atypical antipsychotics are also used. Benzodiazepines alone may be insufficient for severe psychotic agitation but are good for anxious or stimulant-induced agitation.

question: A 45-year-old male with a history of alcohol abuse was found down in his apartment by his landlord. He reportedly has been drinking heavily for weeks. On exam he is confused tremulous and diaphoretic. He is picking at his clothes and appears to be responding to visual hallucinations. Vital signs are BP 168/102 HR 124 RR 22 SpO2 96% Temp 100.8F. You recognize this presentation as alcohol withdrawal with possible delirium tremens. What is the first-line medication class for treatment of alcohol withdrawal and why?
option_a: Phenobarbital because it is a long-acting barbiturate
option_b: Haloperidol because it treats the hallucinations
option_c: Benzodiazepines because they enhance GABA activity similar to alcohol and prevent progression to seizures and severe DTs
option_d: Beta-blockers because they treat the tachycardia and hypertension
rationale: Benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for alcohol withdrawal. Alcohol enhances GABA-A receptor activity and chronic use leads to receptor downregulation. Abrupt cessation causes CNS hyperexcitability leading to withdrawal symptoms. Benzodiazepines also enhance GABA activity cross-tolerating with alcohol and preventing seizures and progression to delirium tremens. Diazepam or lorazepam are commonly used. Haloperidol can be added for severe agitation but can lower seizure threshold. Beta-blockers treat symptoms but do not prevent seizures.

question: You are performing RSI on a 55-year-old trauma patient with severe head injury. GCS is 6 with a blown right pupil. He is combative and hypoxic with SpO2 85%. Vital signs are BP 178/100 HR 58 RR 8 irregular. You plan to use etomidate for induction. After preoxygenation you administer etomidate 0.3 mg/kg IV. The patient becomes apneic and relaxed but you notice brief myoclonic movements of his extremities. What is the explanation for these movements and should you be concerned?
option_a: The patient is seizing and needs benzodiazepines immediately
option_b: The myoclonic movements are a known benign side effect of etomidate and are not seizures
option_c: The etomidate is not working and you need additional sedation
option_d: The patient is experiencing malignant hyperthermia
rationale: Myoclonus is a common side effect of etomidate occurring in 50-80% of patients. These are brief involuntary muscle jerks that are not seizures and do not require treatment. They result from disinhibition of subcortical structures. Etomidate is otherwise an excellent induction agent for hemodynamically unstable patients and those with head injury because it has minimal cardiovascular effects and may decrease ICP. The main concern with etomidate is transient adrenal suppression which is generally not clinically significant with single-dose use.

question: During RSI of a trauma patient you administer ketamine 2 mg/kg IV for induction followed by rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg. Intubation is successful with confirmed placement. Post-intubation the patient has copious oral secretions requiring frequent suctioning and on the monitor you note the heart rate has increased from 80 to 115. The blood pressure has increased from 120/80 to 150/95. Are these findings concerning and what is the explanation?
option_a: Yes these findings suggest allergic reaction to ketamine
option_b: No these are expected effects of ketamine which causes sympathetic stimulation and increased secretions
option_c: Yes the tachycardia suggests the patient is in pain and needs more sedation
option_d: Yes the hypertension suggests cerebral herniation
rationale: Ketamine causes sympathetic stimulation resulting in increased heart rate blood pressure and cardiac output making it useful in hypotensive patients but potentially problematic in uncontrolled hypertension or cardiac ischemia. It also increases secretions which is why atropine or glycopyrrolate pretreatment is sometimes given though this is controversial. Ketamine provides excellent analgesia and amnesia. Previous concerns about ketamine raising ICP have largely been dispelled and it is now considered safe in head injury when hypotension is avoided.

question: You are preparing to intubate a 70-year-old male with respiratory failure from pneumonia. He has a history of COPD chronic kidney disease and hypertension. His medications include albuterol fluticasone lisinopril and amlodipine. He was recently hospitalized for an acute kidney injury with creatinine of 4.2. His current potassium is unknown. Vital signs are BP 142/88 HR 108 RR 32 SpO2 86% on 15L NRB. You are considering paralytic agents for RSI. Why might succinylcholine be a poor choice in this patient?
option_a: Succinylcholine is contraindicated in patients with COPD
option_b: Succinylcholine causes hyperkalemia and patients with renal failure may have elevated baseline potassium
option_c: Succinylcholine is ineffective in elderly patients
option_d: Succinylcholine is contraindicated with ACE inhibitors
rationale: Succinylcholine causes depolarization of muscle cells which releases intracellular potassium typically raising serum potassium by 0.5-1.0 mEq/L. In patients with renal failure who may already have hyperkalemia this additional increase can cause cardiac arrhythmias including ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest. Other conditions with hyperkalemia risk include burns greater than 24 hours crush injuries denervation injuries and prolonged immobilization. Rocuronium is a safer alternative in these patients.

question: A 28-year-old male firefighter was rescued from a structure fire 48 hours ago with 40% total body surface area burns to his torso and extremities. He has been in the burn ICU but is now being transferred and becomes agitated with worsening respiratory distress requiring intubation during transport. The flight nurse suggests using succinylcholine for RSI because of its rapid onset. Why is succinylcholine contraindicated in this patient?
option_a: Succinylcholine is inactivated by burned tissue
option_b: Burn patients develop extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors leading to massive potassium release when succinylcholine is given
option_c: Succinylcholine causes fluid shifts in burn patients
option_d: Succinylcholine is contraindicated with burn medications
rationale: Burns greater than 24 hours old cause proliferation of extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors on muscle cells. When succinylcholine causes depolarization massive potassium efflux occurs from these abnormal receptors potentially causing fatal hyperkalemia. This risk begins around 24 hours post-burn and can persist for months. Similar receptor proliferation occurs with denervation injuries spinal cord injury prolonged immobilization and severe infections. Rocuronium or another non-depolarizing agent should be used instead.

question: You have successfully intubated a patient using RSI with etomidate and rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg. Twenty minutes into transport the ventilator alarms high pressure and the patient appears to be biting on the endotracheal tube. The SpO2 is dropping to 88%. What is the most likely explanation and what should you do?
option_a: The rocuronium is wearing off and you should administer additional sedation and possible redosing of paralytic
option_b: The patient has developed tension pneumothorax
option_c: The ETT has become obstructed with secretions
option_d: The patient is having a seizure
rationale: Rocuronium at RSI doses of 1.2 mg/kg lasts approximately 45-60 minutes but the duration can vary. At 20 minutes the paralysis may be waning especially if a lower dose was used. The patient regaining muscle tone and biting on the tube suggests paralytic wearing off. First ensure adequate sedation as awareness under paralysis is traumatic. Then consider additional paralytic dosing if needed for ventilation. Always rule out other causes like pneumothorax or tube problems. Continuous sedation and possible paralytic infusion may be needed for transport.

question: During RSI of a 22-year-old male with status asthmaticus you administer succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg for paralysis. As you are preparing to intubate you notice the patient has severe jaw rigidity and the masseter muscles are extremely tight making it difficult to open his mouth. His temperature is 99.8F. What is your immediate concern and what should you do?
option_a: This is normal succinylcholine effect wait 60 seconds and it will resolve
option_b: This may represent early malignant hyperthermia you should abandon succinylcholine use not give more and prepare to treat MH if it develops
option_c: The patient is not adequately paralyzed and needs more succinylcholine
option_d: The patient is having a dystonic reaction and needs diphenhydramine
rationale: Masseter muscle rigidity after succinylcholine may be an early sign of malignant hyperthermia a rare but life-threatening hypermetabolic crisis of skeletal muscle. It occurs in genetically susceptible individuals exposed to triggering agents including succinylcholine and volatile anesthetics. Early signs include masseter rigidity tachycardia rising temperature and elevated EtCO2. Treatment includes discontinuing triggering agents hyperventilation cooling and dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV. Mortality without treatment exceeds 70%.

question: You intubated a patient using rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg thirty minutes ago. The intubation was unexpectedly difficult and you now realize the patient has an undiagnosed anatomic abnormality that makes ventilation extremely difficult even with the ETT in place. You are having trouble maintaining adequate ventilation and medical control advises considering reversal of paralysis to allow the patient to breathe spontaneously while maintaining the airway. What reversal agent can immediately reverse rocuronium?
option_a: Neostigmine 0.05 mg/kg with glycopyrrolate
option_b: Sugammadex 16 mg/kg for immediate reversal of rocuronium
option_c: Naloxone 2 mg IV
option_d: Flumazenil 0.2 mg IV
rationale: Sugammadex is a modified gamma-cyclodextrin that encapsulates rocuronium and vecuronium molecules creating a complex that is renally excreted. At 16 mg/kg it provides immediate reversal within 1-3 minutes of even deep neuromuscular blockade from rocuronium. Standard reversal dose is 2-4 mg/kg for moderate blockade. Neostigmine reverses non-depolarizing agents but takes 15-20 minutes and requires anticholinergic co-administration. Naloxone reverses opioids and flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines.

question: You are managing a 58-year-old male in cardiac arrest found in VF. After 3 shocks and 2 rounds of CPR with 2 doses of epinephrine the rhythm remains VF. Per ACLS guidelines what antiarrhythmic medication and dose should you administer at this point?
option_a: Lidocaine 100 mg IV push
option_b: Amiodarone 300 mg IV push
option_c: Magnesium sulfate 2 grams IV push
option_d: Adenosine 12 mg IV push
rationale: Amiodarone is the first-line antiarrhythmic for shock-refractory VF and pulseless VT. The initial dose is 300 mg IV push which can be followed by a second dose of 150 mg. Amiodarone works through multiple mechanisms including sodium potassium and calcium channel blockade as well as beta-blocking effects. Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg is an alternative if amiodarone is not available. Magnesium is indicated for torsades de pointes. Adenosine has no role in cardiac arrest.

question: You are resuscitating a 45-year-old female found in cardiac arrest. The initial rhythm was ventricular fibrillation. After defibrillation the rhythm has changed to PEA with a narrow complex at 82 bpm. You continue high-quality CPR. The 12-lead obtained before arrest showed ST elevation in V1-V4. What is the epinephrine dose and interval for this patient and when during the resuscitation should it be given?
option_a: Epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg IV every 3-5 minutes given during CPR
option_b: Epinephrine 1 mg IV every 3-5 minutes given as soon as possible for non-shockable rhythm
option_c: Epinephrine 1 mg IV every 10 minutes with rhythm checks
option_d: Epinephrine 0.1 mg IV every 5 minutes
rationale: In PEA and asystole epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO should be given as soon as IV/IO access is established and repeated every 3-5 minutes during resuscitation. For shockable rhythms epinephrine is given after the first shock fails to restore perfusion. Epinephrine's alpha-adrenergic effects increase coronary perfusion pressure and myocardial blood flow during CPR. The dose is fixed at 1 mg for adults not weight-based as the 0.01 mg/kg would be.

question: During resuscitation of a cardiac arrest patient you note the rhythm has changed to a wide complex bradycardia at 30 bpm with very tall peaked T waves and absent P waves. The QRS is approximately 0.20 seconds. A family member arrives and states the patient has kidney disease and missed dialysis this week. What medication should you administer immediately?
option_a: Epinephrine 1 mg IV
option_b: Calcium chloride 1 gram IV slowly
option_c: Sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV
option_d: Atropine 1 mg IV
rationale: This presentation suggests severe hyperkalemia causing cardiac arrest. The ECG findings of peaked T waves widened QRS absent P waves and bradycardia are classic for hyperkalemia. Calcium chloride 1 gram IV given slowly over 2-3 minutes stabilizes the cardiac membrane and should be given immediately. This does not lower potassium but protects against fatal arrhythmias. After calcium give sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV and insulin 10 units with dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly. Dialysis is definitive treatment.

question: You are resuscitating a 32-year-old female who was pulled from a swimming pool. She was submerged for an estimated 5 minutes. CPR was started by bystanders. On your arrival she is in cardiac arrest with a rhythm showing fine VF. After 2 shocks the rhythm has changed to asystole. What is unique about the resuscitation of drowning victims and what should be prioritized?
option_a: Drowning victims should not be shocked because water in the lungs conducts electricity
option_b: Drowning is a hypoxic event and ventilation and oxygenation should be prioritized along with standard ACLS
option_c: Drowning victims should receive double-dose epinephrine
option_d: Drowning victims do not respond to resuscitation and efforts should be limited
rationale: Drowning causes cardiac arrest through hypoxia rather than primary cardiac causes. Therefore oxygenation and ventilation are particularly important. Standard ACLS algorithms should be followed including defibrillation for shockable rhythms. There is no evidence to support higher dose epinephrine. Survival with good neurologic outcome is possible even after prolonged submersion especially in cold water due to the protective effects of hypothermia. Resuscitation efforts should be prolonged in drowning victims.

question: A 70-year-old male collapses at a shopping mall. Bystander CPR is in progress when you arrive. The AED has delivered 2 shocks for VF. You take over care and note continued VF on your monitor. You continue CPR establish IV access and administer epinephrine 1 mg IV. After the 3rd shock and amiodarone 300 mg the patient has ROSC. He is unconscious with BP 88/50 HR 102 RR 8 agonal and SpO2 90%. After securing the airway what vasopressor would be most appropriate for his hypotension?
option_a: Dopamine 2-5 mcg/kg/min to start
option_b: Epinephrine or norepinephrine infusion titrated to maintain SBP greater than 90 and MAP greater than 65
option_c: Phenylephrine 100-200 mcg IV boluses
option_d: Vasopressin 20 units IV push
rationale: Post-cardiac arrest syndrome frequently includes myocardial dysfunction and vasodilation causing hypotension. Current guidelines recommend targeting SBP greater than 90 mmHg and MAP greater than 65 mmHg to maintain organ perfusion. Epinephrine or norepinephrine infusions are first-line with typical starting doses of 0.1-0.5 mcg/kg/min titrated to effect. Dopamine is an alternative. Phenylephrine lacks inotropic effects and vasopressin is not first-line for post-arrest hypotension.

question: A 19-year-old college student is brought from a party where she reportedly took several pills. Her friends say she is not acting like herself. On exam she is confused tachycardic and has dilated pupils. She is sweating profusely and her jaw is clenched with teeth grinding. She keeps saying she loves everyone. Her temperature is 103.8F. Vital signs are BP 158/94 HR 142 RR 24 SpO2 98%. You suspect MDMA ecstasy intoxication with sympathomimetic toxidrome. What is the most appropriate medication for this patient?
option_a: Activated charcoal 50 grams oral
option_b: Benzodiazepines such as midazolam 5 mg IV for sedation and diazepam for hyperthermia
option_c: Haloperidol 10 mg IM for the psychotic symptoms
option_d: Propranolol 1 mg IV for the tachycardia
rationale: MDMA causes a combined sympathomimetic and serotonergic toxidrome. Benzodiazepines are first-line treatment addressing agitation tachycardia hypertension and hyperthermia by reducing CNS stimulation and muscle hyperactivity. Active cooling is also essential. Beta-blockers are contraindicated due to risk of unopposed alpha stimulation worsening hypertension. Haloperidol can lower seizure threshold and worsen hyperthermia. If serotonin syndrome features predominate cyproheptadine may be added.

question: Paramedics respond to a 45-year-old male who reportedly took an entire bottle of his blood pressure medication in a suicide attempt 2 hours ago. The pill bottle shows metoprolol 50 mg with 30 tablets dispensed 2 days ago. On exam he is lethargic with cool extremities. Vital signs are BP 72/40 HR 42 RR 14 SpO2 94% and glucose is 118. He responds to voice but is confused. ECG shows sinus bradycardia at 42 without conduction blocks. What is the first-line antidote for beta-blocker overdose?
option_a: Flumazenil 0.2 mg IV
option_b: Glucagon 3-5 mg IV bolus followed by infusion
option_c: Calcium chloride 1 gram IV
option_d: Atropine 0.5 mg IV
rationale: Glucagon is the first-line antidote for beta-blocker overdose. It activates adenylyl cyclase through a non-beta-receptor mechanism increasing intracellular cAMP and providing inotropic and chronotropic effects that bypass the beta-blockade. Initial dose is 3-5 mg IV bolus which can be repeated. If effective a continuous infusion of 2-5 mg per hour should be started. Additional treatments include calcium chloride high-dose insulin with glucose and vasopressors. Atropine is often ineffective in significant beta-blocker overdose.

question: A 52-year-old female with a history of depression presents after intentionally ingesting unknown quantity of her medication approximately 1 hour ago. The pill bottle shows amitriptyline 50 mg. She is now drowsy and confused with dry mouth and dilated pupils. Her skin is warm and dry. Vital signs are BP 88/58 HR 128 RR 16 SpO2 96%. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with QRS duration of 140 ms and a rightward axis. She has one brief seizure witnessed by EMS. What is the most important medication intervention for tricyclic antidepressant overdose with QRS widening?
option_a: Physostigmine 2 mg IV to reverse anticholinergic effects
option_b: Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV push to narrow the QRS
option_c: Magnesium sulfate 2 grams IV for the wide QRS
option_d: Activated charcoal 50 grams if she can protect her airway
rationale: Sodium bicarbonate is the primary treatment for TCA cardiotoxicity. TCAs block sodium channels in the heart causing QRS widening and increasing arrhythmia risk. Bicarbonate provides sodium to overcome channel blockade and alkalinization decreases drug binding. The goal is to maintain pH 7.50-7.55 and narrow QRS to less than 100 ms. Physostigmine is contraindicated because it can cause seizures and worsen cardiac toxicity. Benzodiazepines are used for seizures. Activated charcoal may help if given early but airway protection is essential.

question: A 28-year-old male is found unresponsive at a known drug house. On exam he has pinpoint pupils RR of 4 and shallow respirations. SpO2 is 78%. He has track marks on both arms. You ventilate him with BVM and administer naloxone 0.4 mg IV. He awakens and becomes combative refusing care and demanding to leave. He states he used his normal heroin dose and does not understand why this happened. A friend mentions the heroin may have been contaminated with something. Given the increasing prevalence of fentanyl-contaminated heroin what should you be particularly concerned about with this patient?
option_a: Fentanyl has a shorter half-life so he will not resedate
option_b: Fentanyl has a longer half-life than naloxone and he is at high risk for re-sedation and respiratory arrest
option_c: Fentanyl is not reversed by naloxone
option_d: Fentanyl contamination does not change management
rationale: Fentanyl and its analogs have become increasingly common adulterants in heroin and other street drugs. While naloxone does reverse fentanyl effects there are several concerns. Some fentanyl analogs may require higher doses of naloxone. More importantly fentanyl and especially longer-acting analogs like carfentanil have durations that exceed naloxone's 30-90 minute half-life. Patients are at high risk for re-sedation and recurrent respiratory depression. Extended observation or naloxone infusion may be needed.

question: A 35-year-old farmer is brought in by coworkers who found him confused in the barn where pesticides are stored. He was reportedly mixing chemicals without proper protection. On exam he is diaphoretic with excessive salivation vomiting and diarrhea. He has urinated on himself. His pupils are pinpoint. He has diffuse wheezing and muscle fasciculations. Heart rate is 48 and he is becoming increasingly lethargic. Vital signs are BP 88/54 HR 48 RR 28 labored SpO2 88%. You recognize organophosphate poisoning with cholinergic crisis. What is the first-line medication and dose?
option_a: Physostigmine 2 mg IV to reverse the cholinergic effects
option_b: Atropine 2-4 mg IV repeated every 5-10 minutes until secretions dry and dosed to clinical effect
option_c: Pralidoxime 1 gram IV as the only treatment needed
option_d: Naloxone 2 mg IV for the pinpoint pupils
rationale: Atropine is the first-line treatment for organophosphate or carbamate poisoning blocking muscarinic effects. The initial dose is 2-4 mg IV and should be doubled every 5-10 minutes until secretions dry up the key clinical endpoint. Massive doses may be required sometimes hundreds of milligrams. Pralidoxime 2-PAM reactivates acetylcholinesterase and should be given with atropine but atropine takes priority. Decontamination with removal of clothing and skin washing is essential to prevent ongoing absorption and protect providers.

question: A 24-year-old female presents with headache dizziness confusion and cherry-red skin coloring. She was found in her running car in a closed garage. Multiple family members have similar symptoms. On exam she is confused and tachypneic. Vital signs are BP 138/84 HR 118 RR 28 SpO2 99% on room air. You recognize carbon monoxide poisoning. Why is the SpO2 reading misleading and what is the treatment?
option_a: The SpO2 is accurate and CO poisoning causes high oxygen levels
option_b: Standard pulse oximetry cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin from oxyhemoglobin so SpO2 appears falsely normal. Treatment is high-flow 100% oxygen via NRB
option_c: The SpO2 is low in CO poisoning
option_d: Treatment is hyperbaric oxygen only which you should arrange
rationale: Standard pulse oximetry measures light absorption and cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin from oxyhemoglobin giving falsely reassuring readings. CO-oximetry can measure specific hemoglobin species. Treatment is high-flow 100% oxygen which reduces the half-life of carboxyhemoglobin from 5-6 hours on room air to 60-90 minutes on 100% O2. Hyperbaric oxygen further reduces half-life to 20-30 minutes and is indicated for severe poisoning including LOC neurological symptoms pregnancy and COHb greater than 25%.

question: You respond to a house fire where a 40-year-old male was pulled from a smoke-filled room. He is confused and hypotensive with BP 78/50 despite fluid resuscitation. He has soot in his airway and singed nasal hair. Lab values from the hospital where medical control is located show elevated lactate and metabolic acidosis with normal carboxyhemoglobin. The physician suspects cyanide poisoning from smoke inhalation. What is the preferred antidote for cyanide poisoning in the prehospital setting?
option_a: Amyl nitrite pearls crushed and inhaled
option_b: Hydroxocobalamin 5 grams IV over 15 minutes
option_c: Sodium thiosulfate 12.5 grams IV
option_d: Methylene blue 1 mg/kg IV
rationale: Hydroxocobalamin marketed as Cyanokit is the preferred prehospital antidote for cyanide poisoning. It binds cyanide to form cyanocobalamin vitamin B12 which is renally excreted. The dose is 5 grams IV over 15 minutes which can be repeated once. It is safer than the older nitrite/thiosulfate kit which induces methemoglobinemia. Hydroxocobalamin causes red discoloration of skin and urine and can interfere with some lab tests but is well-tolerated. High-flow oxygen should also be administered.

question: A 16-year-old female was found by her parents with an empty bottle of diphenhydramine. The bottle originally contained 100 tablets of 25 mg each. The ingestion was approximately 2 hours ago. She is now hallucinating seeing spiders on the walls and has attempted to get up and run. On exam she has dry mouth flushed dry skin dilated pupils and is tachycardic. She is disoriented and agitated. Vital signs are BP 118/78 HR 132 RR 20 SpO2 99% Temp 100.2F. You recognize anticholinergic toxidrome. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
option_a: Physostigmine 1-2 mg slow IV push as the specific antidote
option_b: Supportive care with benzodiazepines for agitation IV fluids and monitoring reserving physostigmine for severe refractory cases
option_c: Activated charcoal 50 grams immediately
option_d: Flumazenil 0.2 mg IV
rationale: Anticholinergic toxidrome treatment is primarily supportive. Benzodiazepines are first-line for agitation as they are safe and effective. Physostigmine is the specific antidote but is reserved for severe cases with refractory seizures or severe agitation unresponsive to benzodiazepines. Physostigmine can cause seizures bradycardia and asystole especially in TCA overdose. Activated charcoal may help if given early but is difficult to administer in an agitated patient and aspiration risk must be considered.

question: A 22-year-old female with type 1 diabetes presents with altered mental status. Her college roommate states she has not eaten today and gave herself her normal insulin dose this morning. She then became progressively confused and is now barely responsive. On exam she is diaphoretic tremulous and pale with a GCS of 9. Vital signs are BP 102/68 HR 118 RR 18 SpO2 98%. Blood glucose is 28 mg/dL. IV access is difficult and you are unable to establish a line after two attempts. What is the most appropriate next treatment?
option_a: Continue attempting IV access for dextrose administration
option_b: Administer glucagon 1 mg IM while continuing to attempt IV access
option_c: Administer oral glucose gel
option_d: Administer dextrose rectally
rationale: When IV access cannot be obtained in hypoglycemic patients glucagon 1 mg IM or SC is the appropriate treatment. Glucagon stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis raising blood glucose within 10-20 minutes. It is less effective in patients with depleted glycogen stores such as alcoholics or malnourished patients. Oral glucose is contraindicated in patients with altered mental status who cannot protect their airway. Intranasal glucagon is also now available. IO access is another option for dextrose administration.

question: A 58-year-old male with type 2 diabetes presents with confusion and weakness. His wife states he has been sick with a cold for several days and has not been eating well. His medications include metformin and glipizide. He appears dehydrated with poor skin turgor and dry mucous membranes. Vital signs are BP 98/62 HR 108 RR 16 SpO2 96%. Blood glucose is 48 mg/dL. You establish IV access and prepare to administer dextrose. What is the appropriate initial dextrose formulation and dose for this adult patient?
option_a: D5W 500 mL IV bolus
option_b: D10W 100-250 mL IV or D50W 25 grams 50 mL IV
option_c: D50W 100 grams 200 mL IV
option_d: D25W 12.5 grams 50 mL IV
rationale: For adult hypoglycemia the traditional treatment is D50W 25 grams which is 50 mL of 50% dextrose given IV push. Alternatively D10W 100-250 mL can be used which is less hyperosmolar and may be preferred especially if there is any concern about IV infiltration as D50 can cause tissue necrosis if extravasated. D10 also allows easier titration. Recheck glucose 5-10 minutes after administration and repeat as needed. The dose for children is D25W 2-4 mL/kg and for neonates D10W 2-4 mL/kg.

question: A 45-year-old female with a history of hypothyroidism presents in an obtunded state. Her husband states she ran out of her thyroid medication 2 months ago and could not afford refills. She has been increasingly fatigued and confused over the past week. On exam she has periorbital edema dry coarse skin and sparse hair. Her voice is hoarse and deep. She is bradycardic and hypothermic. Vital signs are BP 88/58 HR 48 RR 12 SpO2 92% Temp 94.8F. You recognize myxedema coma. What is the prehospital treatment priority?
option_a: Administer levothyroxine IV immediately
option_b: Supportive care including passive warming airway management IV fluids and glucose check with transport to hospital for thyroid hormone replacement
option_c: Administer hydrocortisone 100 mg IV for presumed adrenal insufficiency
option_d: Active rapid rewarming with heated blankets
rationale: Myxedema coma is severe hypothyroidism that is a medical emergency. Prehospital treatment is primarily supportive as IV thyroid hormone is not typically available. Priorities include airway management as these patients are at risk for respiratory failure gentle passive rewarming avoiding active rewarming which can cause vasodilation and cardiovascular collapse IV fluids for hypotension and dextrose for hypoglycemia which is common. Patients may have concomitant adrenal insufficiency and steroids may be given in hospital but this is not first-line prehospital.

question: A 32-year-old female with Addison disease presents with weakness abdominal pain nausea and vomiting. She has had a viral illness for the past 3 days. Her medical alert bracelet indicates she takes hydrocortisone daily. She appears acutely ill with dry mucous membranes. Vital signs are BP 78/52 HR 124 RR 22 SpO2 97%. Blood glucose is 62 mg/dL. Her skin appears darker than expected especially in skin creases. You suspect adrenal crisis. What is the essential medication and dose for this patient?
option_a: Dexamethasone 4 mg IV
option_b: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV or if unavailable any available corticosteroid at stress dose
option_c: Epinephrine 1 mg IV for the hypotension
option_d: Normal saline 2 liters is sufficient treatment
rationale: Adrenal crisis is life-threatening acute adrenal insufficiency requiring immediate treatment with stress-dose corticosteroids. Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV is preferred because it has both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity. If hydrocortisone is unavailable dexamethasone 4 mg or methylprednisolone 125 mg can be used though they lack mineralocorticoid effects. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with normal saline is also essential as these patients are typically severely volume depleted. Glucose should be supplemented if hypoglycemic.

question: A 67-year-old male with a history of cirrhosis presents with massive hematemesis. He has vomited approximately 500 mL of bright red blood and is continuing to have bloody emesis. He appears pale and diaphoretic. His wife states he was recently diagnosed with esophageal varices. Vital signs are BP 82/50 HR 128 RR 26 SpO2 94%. You establish two large-bore IVs and begin fluid resuscitation. Medical control orders octreotide. What is the mechanism and purpose of octreotide in this patient?
option_a: Octreotide is an antiemetic that will stop the vomiting
option_b: Octreotide is a somatostatin analog that reduces splanchnic blood flow and portal pressure decreasing variceal bleeding
option_c: Octreotide neutralizes stomach acid
option_d: Octreotide replaces clotting factors
rationale: Octreotide is a synthetic somatostatin analog that reduces splanchnic blood flow and portal venous pressure. In variceal hemorrhage it helps reduce bleeding by decreasing blood flow to the varices. The dose is typically 50 mcg IV bolus followed by 50 mcg/hour infusion. It is an adjunct to resuscitation and definitive treatment endoscopy or TIPS. Other treatments for variceal bleeding include IV PPI antibiotics and possibly vasopressin though vasopressin has more side effects.

question: A 28-year-old male with sickle cell disease presents with severe pain in his back chest and legs rated 10 out of 10. He states this feels like his typical pain crisis. He has had multiple previous crises requiring hospitalization. He is in obvious distress writhing in pain. Vital signs are BP 148/92 HR 112 RR 22 SpO2 96% on room air. You establish IV access. What is the appropriate approach to pain management in sickle cell crisis?
option_a: Avoid opioids due to risk of addiction in this patient population
option_b: Administer aggressive opioid analgesia with morphine 0.1-0.15 mg/kg IV or equivalent as sickle cell crises are genuinely painful
option_c: Ketorolac 30 mg IV is sufficient for this pain
option_d: Administer aspirin 325 mg for its anti-sickling properties
rationale: Sickle cell pain crises cause severe vaso-occlusive pain that requires aggressive opioid analgesia. These patients are frequently undertreated due to unfounded concerns about addiction. Morphine hydromorphone or fentanyl are appropriate with doses titrated to pain relief. IV fluids and oxygen are also important components of treatment. NSAIDs may be used as adjuncts but are not sufficient alone for severe crises. Aspirin does not have anti-sickling properties and the sickle hemoglobin is a structural not clotting abnormality.

question: A 72-year-old female presents with sudden onset right-sided weakness and slurred speech that began 45 minutes ago while eating breakfast. Her husband witnessed the onset. She has a history of atrial fibrillation and takes warfarin. On exam she has right facial droop right arm drift and dysarthria. Vital signs are BP 178/96 HR 88 irregular RR 16 SpO2 97%. Blood glucose is 128 mg/dL. This is a stroke alert. What is the blood pressure management strategy for suspected acute ischemic stroke?
option_a: Lower BP immediately to 140/90 with labetalol
option_b: Generally allow permissive hypertension unless BP exceeds 220/120 for non-tPA candidates or 185/110 for potential tPA candidates
option_c: Treat any SBP above 160 with IV nicardipine
option_d: Do not check or treat blood pressure in suspected stroke
rationale: In acute ischemic stroke elevated blood pressure helps maintain perfusion to the ischemic penumbra. Aggressive BP lowering can worsen stroke outcomes. Current guidelines allow permissive hypertension up to 220/120 for patients not receiving thrombolytics. For potential tPA candidates BP must be below 185/110 before and 180/105 after tPA administration. Labetalol or nicardipine are preferred agents when treatment is needed. This patient on warfarin may not be a tPA candidate depending on INR.

question: A 45-year-old male presents with seizure activity witnessed by family. He has a history of epilepsy and his wife states he has not had a seizure in two years since starting his current medication. The seizure began 8 minutes ago and is ongoing. He has tonic-clonic activity and is unresponsive. He is incontinent of urine. Vital signs are BP 168/98 HR 128 RR 8 shallow with cyanosis SpO2 82%. What is the appropriate first-line medication and dose for this patient in status epilepticus?
option_a: Phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV infusion
option_b: Benzodiazepine such as midazolam 10 mg IM or 5 mg IV lorazepam 4 mg IV or diazepam 10 mg IV
option_c: Phenobarbital 20 mg/kg IV
option_d: Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV
rationale: Benzodiazepines are first-line treatment for status epilepticus. They enhance GABA-A receptor activity terminating most seizures rapidly. Options include midazolam 10 mg IM or 5 mg IV lorazepam 4 mg IV or diazepam 10 mg IV. IM midazolam is as effective as IV lorazepam and useful when IV access is difficult. If benzodiazepines fail second-line agents include fosphenytoin phenobarbital levetiracetam or valproate. Airway management and oxygenation are also critical priorities.
